title: CHEAP Dermal Filler...How Do They Do It？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show]

excerpt: How the hell are they charging that [Music] welcome to the ascetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm ray Davis and today we are gonna have our first viral show unfortunately it's the corona virus we're going to talk about and we're also going to talk about hepatitis B C …

segments.text: how the hell are they charging that [Music] welcome to the ascetics mastery show I'm doctor Tim Pierce hi I'm ray Davis and today we are gonna have our first viral show unfortunately it's the corona virus we're going to talk about and we're also going to talk about hepatitis B C and HIV and how it could be spread with certain bad practices what else we can talk about so we're also gonna tackle the thorny question of how the hell are they charging that I see this on Facebook groups all the time you guys asking seriously forty pound for one mil of filler and so yeah we're gonna go there and ask and actually answer the question how are they managing they being competitors may be non meds etc how are they managing to charge so little yes good interesting topics that come up all the time on social media so and what can I respond to be as well as

practitioners so let's dig in what's first okay so let's go there first I think so it really is such a pain point I see you guys all the time saying someone's opened up next to me and they're charging two pence for five mills and I know that how I'm supposed to compete and then what gets loaded in as well is that you guys say I've spent so much on courses I put all my energies into it I'm you don't say this but I can sense that you're really falling in love with this industry and what it could potentially bring to your life and then someone just comes along next to you charges ridiculous price but also they often seem to have a great social media following so it's incredibly triggering and people are literally racking their brains like how can they charge that when we all of us medical people are buying from proper pharmacies and paying you know obviously normal prices so what what's going on so um there are multiple

ways that you can reduce your prices and some of them are absolutely fine there's nothing wrong with them and I don't want to be someone who says there's only one business model you know there's room for LD and there's room for Sainsbury's and you can take that model of I'm gonna have low margins and high volumes and the problem is when you start to cut corners in order to achieve that and that's where I really have an issue with it and that unfortunately is absolutely happening and there's loads of evidence of it you get shared on these Facebook groups and you can find it if you just search for it so it's worth remembering that there are those two components to it some people are just doing legitimately doing a different business model I'm trying to reduce the cost so that get more clients that way unfortunately there's very fine line because one of the things it's worth knowing around business strategy in general is there's only one room there's only one person who can be the cheapest in town anyone else is there for at a disadvantage because you can't you're competing for the niche of cheapest and that can work if you can maintain that but it's a difficult game where the need to cut corners will pop into your mind occasionally

because we're all human and if someone else comes out is a pound cheaper and you've got to cut costs even more some people some of the time not all people will think of a way of doing it a bit cheaper that's not actually in the patient's best interest and those are the ideas we've got to be very careful of and look out for so there are lots of those and one of them would be I mean we did we had a researcher look for buying product online and we found some absolutely shocking numbers so you can actually buy a dermal filler product for $8 for two mils and you can actually pay extra and have it branded if you want to so you can create your own brand I'm not going to share the link of where that's findable but this really blew me away because we pay you know about a hundred pounds per mil to buy it more than that hundred and twenty so if you're trying to compete with someone who's buying it from the internet from an unvalidated source because that's the big difference this is where it gets tricky is that these products are made in an unregulated fashion and they're just made in a factory somewhere probably you know somewhere abroad where there's no testing there's no validation

there's no any claims about how long it lasts or whether it's gotten purities in it none of that's available so literally thinking about it and these the people who making them are also trying to be the cheapest in town so they've also got the same drive to kakuka costs they don't have a brand to think about there's not like they're trying to protect their reputation they're just selling as cheap as possible online making it as cheap as possible trying to cut corners at every point so you just have absolutely no idea what's in it and the chances are the cheapest thing possible is in it because that's the niche they're going for so this is the this is why I hate to be the concept of trying to be the cheapest in town because it drives you down a route where the only way to win is to continuously become cheaper and cheaper which is tricky for most people to stay disciplined and not do something that's not in the interests of your patient now we're supposed to be healthcare professionals I think doing this and you should always put your needs of your patient ahead of cutting costs even if that means you don't treat as many people because you know you're

slightly more expensive so that's the key thing to remember is if you go for the cheapest products in the cheap for the cheapest place in town you're going to you there's a small chance that you're also going to be getting something you're not planning for other ways unfortunately I've heard of clinicians reducing costs is some are diluting it with saline so just mixing the product with even a third of saline will basically reduce your cost by a third so you can't tell on the day it looks pretty much the same they just might lose volume a little bit quicker and you could be getting away with it so it's very hard to tell but I definitely I'm aware of many practitioners who kind of drop that in about little thing that they do which is unfortunate could I ask about that so what is going on with that like can you just pour saline in that it's not going to have any cross infection issues you could do that without a cross infection issues it's possible depends on how you're doing it it's obviously you are messing with the product it's more I don't think it's necessarily a risk for blood-borne diseases it's just a way of spreading the product a bit more thinly it may

have some unintended consequences like the product will probably diffuse slightly it's worth knowing that hyaluronic acid the actual syringe is mainly saline anyway so you have the best basically have a sugar that holds moisture that's kind of what hyaluronic acid is so giving it a bit more moisture to hold isn't that tricky it won't look different it probably won't even feel that different to inject and on the day and maybe the first few weeks afterwards will be quite similar but it isn't as the manufacturers intended it so you may not get quite what you're paying for just from my perspective as you know a non clinical person that's I think that's out of order because you are giving this person who's paying their money no matter how much you're charging he's charging a pound or it's free it's still a relationship as a relationships because contracts gone on they're an emotional one under clinical one where you are basically saying this is what you're getting and then they're not getting it I don't know I mean all those people I wonder are they saying by the way I've looked into it and it's okay I'm diluting your product actually I

don't think there's anything wrong with that if you're taking that approach yeah but are they well some will most probably aren't because the real benefit is the cost saving the real benefit is not to the patient in most cases now there is an argument I have heard it said around you know certain areas of the face that maybe you'll get less edema if you've mixed it up with say light there's no science behind it so there are reasons and I have no objection to this at all if you think you've learned something that fundamentally is true about the product that you believe is true and you may not have a big study but you think that's how it works better and you're open with your patients and you do something to the products like lots of people will mix lidocaine with it in the old days especially there's nothing wrong with doing that if you're open to it there's nothing unethical you may not have science back in you but it's not a moral failing whereas the that unfortunately the very fine line between that and thinking hang on I'm saving a third of my product here my product cost the main cost of your business is 30% less than my competition that's that you know that's going to be tempting for a lot of people to do that and interestingly we haven't spoken about this but there was a colleague put

a link on to the clothes skin Vemma training group yesterday about a case a GMC case that hadn't gone in the way of the doctor because he was and he was using as a law or another botox alternative and wasn't open about that and was saying the patient which I'm sure a lot of people will do that not meaning to be horrible or unethical or anything they're just kind of talking about it as boh to us because that's what we all talk about it as and yeah so it's very interesting so the case there is you have you've told me I'm getting one drug yeah giving me a different one even though it's fair is also fine drugs but yeah but it's not about whether it's a fine drug or not it's the technicality of the fact that it's like prescribing someone an anti giving them an antibiotic from a legal perspective yes that may not be how we all think about it but that's worth knowing that's a really good tip for you guys if you are using different products and refer to them by their names otherwise when you if you end up in a court case and they say you said you treated me with Botox and it turns out to be as a little remote good sure that could be a major

flaw in your and your argument so there's no shame in doing that just do it just telling patients don't mind if you oh if you're open about it you give them the reasons why you've chosen that product and they won't mind at all it's about not lying about which product you're using so there anything else you want to say about the dilution thing not much on dilution the most dangerous of all the ways I've seen people saving costs is by sharing syringes and this comes up on I mean people are really brazen this does come from the non regulated side of the market where they will share literally on a Facebook post I've got someone coming in for treatment does anyone want to share the other half of mil and then you have sometimes even see videos on Instagram of it being done so you can actually see you know a couple of people in the room one of these minor celebrities who does injecting was doing without gloves on so you've now got no gloves on same person two different people having that having a procedure with the same product now I do think most people who've got any kind of education will know they're not going to use the same needle but they're not realizing that there's a really similar risk with blood-borne diseases such as

HIV hepatitis B and possibly HIV that if your work surfaces are contaminated because you and your syringe is a work surface so you're touching that patients bleed if you're not wearing gloves it's even worse because you're probably not washing your hands in between as well so you're injecting one person getting a bit of blood you can probably barely see it might be a tiny streak on the needle and two minutes later you're injecting the next person fair enough you change the needle but you're touching the same surface and these viruses last a long time hepatitis C will live on a surface for three weeks before it's no longer able to contaminate someone hepatitis B is about a week HIV within a dried area of blood can last five to seven days now you've got to have a fair amount of blood to do that but it can certainly last ten minutes in a drop of blood so all of these are potentially transferable for people who are not keeping them basically having a proper clinical workspace where you're being very clean with you patient having their own syringe their own environment basically so this is a big risk there has been one

case of HIV being spread that's known about because they'll be more that aren't known about that was rounded Erminia derma roller treatment I think so quite a bloody procedure but using the same Demerol or in different people obviously it's risky but I think it's a matter of time with bad clinical practice before that happens just a member of the public and I feel like what I'm imagining someone who's unregulated so for example a beauty therapist or whoever is doing this treatment they might think oh well I've changed the needle what's the problem you know stop going on about it you know doctor term but actually I think the reason that they can get away with that kind of attitude is because they've got nothing to lose you know there's no if that patient gets HIV like what is there's no redress against this person there's the worries if you do something bad there's something you've got something to lose which is your GMC pin and really this is why it's so wrong this is at the heart of the whole unregulated people versus regulated people in our industry is that there's

nowhere to go they've got nothing to lose really I mean maybe reputation but you can overcome that as we've seen yeah I don't want to stated that I'm sure people care about people as a general rule the problem is we know from talking to the people that we trained healthcare professionals have anxiety about losing their pin they're constantly thinking about it and it's actually woven into the culture it becomes I was thinking about how working in a hospital you kind of get indoctrinated into a way of thinking about patient safety and about your how you fit into that world and when you first start you don't quite get it and then you get so many conversations with other clinicians who like oh you know you've got about to defend that action in court and what are you going to say if someone asks you this whole can you defend your practice is something as woven into you into anyone who's an independent practitioner which just isn't something it's needed in if you're in a part of the non-regulated world so even good people basically I think are quite likely to have bad practice because they're not habitual izing this way to minimize risk constantly and to fear the impact on their own personal lives if

again if it goes wrong so I absolutely agree with units if there is a sense of nothing to lose but I don't think that means these people are necessary cavalier it's just that they just don't have the same neat the same need to systematically go through those processes of minimizing risk because it's not part of the culture of how they think and we can see that because it's on Instagram you can literally watch people doing horrendous things that are let's scare the crap out of me basically when I see it being done the injection techniques the lack of safety the lack of gloves to lack of you know basic cleanliness but it's not to say that they actually don't care about their patients they're just oblivious like it's basically that dunning-kruger effect which I spoke about recently which is they don't know what they don't know they feel like good people just trying to make a difference but they just don't do not know what they're missing out and that's where the risk to patient comes so people might be watching us here thinking well it's all very well you know you saying that we shouldn't be the cheapest in town but I live in a deprived area my clients are always challenging me on my prices and

it's tricky with certain people to get a lift if they're not having a few syringes so you know it's right for you doctor Tim with your big shiny clinic what about me how can people watching this here market themselves and to not be the cheapest in town well the biggest piece of advice I can give use don't commoditize yourself by selling syringes even packages of this as well because as soon as you say I'm selling us a mil of filler what's the difference between you and the person down the road selling a mellow filler there is no difference to the uneducated which is everyone who's not in this industry the difference between you know a filler that might be called who knows like Viva filler versus you know H an fda-approved product it means there's no difference you can stick any brand you like it might be a brand that you made up and you bought on the chinese website and had branded up yourself there's no way to tell so you've got to sell more than the thing you're injecting you've got to sell yourself you've got to make yourself the unique selling point so that and the only way you can do that is

by delivering lots of value to the people that you treat like really caring about them making the best difference you can educating them along the way and as soon as you start to make yourself the holder of a syringe of filler versus the human being behind it yeah you are you're gonna Your Honor on a way to race to the bottom you're gonna have to compete in price you're gonna have to talk about mills you can have to look for cheaper products to compete because there's no difference between you it's like the word commodity comes from grain buying metals like precious metals or whatever it's the thing has the value not the person who dug it up and we don't want to be competing in that area because the honest truth is the real value is in the practitioner they're the person who looks after them they're you are the person who will look after them if they have a complication you're the person who gives them the advice you're the person who should be owning the risks that they take to make sure that they get the result that actually makes them happy and that is that takes a unique individual it doesn't take a syringe full of hyaluronic acid so don't market yourself as the holder of a syringe of filler

you're much better than that it's so true and a colleague last night on a group Richard he was saying he gave the example of a restaurant so if you go to a Michelin star restaurant you know of course you know that if you went into the kitchen the carrots would be pretty much the same carrots that you can buy anywhere I mean it might be like organic or something but it's not much that much difference what you're buying in that Michelin star restaurant is the Michelin star chef and the experience and the amazing caring but not too intrusive waiters and you know that the booking service is great and everything and one thing I'm really keen to get across is that even if you're watching this thinking I'm not a Michelin star restaurant you know I don't do all the areas of the face yet and I'm not even particularly you know sure that I'm as experienced as I need to be actually think about all the other value adds you present for example are you really caring are you talking to your patients you know you've just had a divorce and giving them that gorgeous love and being

funny on your social media or you know all these kinds of things you know are you informative do you give great videos on your social media that help people through their journey those things are not commodities most people aren't doing them step up give value and then let people know and your marketing that you are doing this let people know about your values let people know that you don't over treat that you won't just go for the quickest bargain honestly like that stuff means so much I think that's the way to decompose and actually did not have to give the cheapest prices in town yeah you have to give more of yourself and which is what I think the reason people become commodities is that they don't actually necessarily value they haven't realized where they fit into all this and it took me a while to do this at the beginning as well because I remember I'm getting feedback from people and then realizing what I was doing differently because before then I thought everyone was doing the same thing and you might have that yourself where you think there is no difference between me and the practitioner down the road it's only when your clients come back and say you know what I went somewhere else and they

didn't give you give me half the information they didn't even do a proper consultation like you do and you're thinking I thought everyone was doing what I doing and they're not so you need to get that message out in your marketing so that people can make a rational choice and some of this comes from people not believing that other that the consumer actually will make a rational choice you've got to have faith in the consumer that if they understand the value of what you're offering they'll make a logical choice which might be their cost a bit more but they're getting something important to them so I don't know why it happens in this industry more than many others but most of us will have a 700-pound phone in our pocket that is the best phone we could find because that's really important to us not everyone and but if everyone had the mindset that it was only the cheapest phone they would survive we'd all be still carrying around Nokia's that did nothing so realizing that if you provide something of value people will pay ten times as much if you can provide ten times the value should lift your eyes to the horizon and then you can start to innovate around what really matters to your patients instead of innovating around cutting corners which

is what happens when you go for the other end of the market you've got but you've got to get still and think what is my value and the best way to do that is to ask your customers say oh why did you come to me I'm just curious you don't have to be weird about it just say oh you know I'm you know I've been following a marketing person online and they said it was a good idea to ask you know why did he come to me and then from there you can actually get clear on your value start talking about that in your marketing and you no longer are a commodity you're no longer that bag of grain or whatever it might be so it's a really good question why did you come to me people will give you real insight into how you were perceived in the outside world and a really great question I'd definitely recommend that you mustn't think that people are there by accident virtually no one is there just because they just found the first person on Instagram something you've done has caused them to come or something that they perceive that you are and that gives you really good feedback about how to build the next part of your business and it may not always be good it can just be while you're the closest that's fine that's

still an issue you can dominate no one else can be closer to that person but look for the quality example so you seem really caring I saw you wrote something that made me think I understand fillers better so you informing them whatever it is that you're doing try and look for the ones that are around you adding value and then do more of that because it's hard easy for us to say but when you experience a client who's booked in saying I read a blog that you wrote on like Hello Botox or something that we did then you're like oh wow that actually helps someone make a decision I'm going to do more of that it'll drive you to do more so Tim coronavirus how can we as practitioners deal with all of the noise that's out there about coronavirus you know our customers might cancel their appointment you know we want to make sure that we're doing everything like kind of safety wise an infection wise all the rest of it what would be your the skinny on how to approach this well first thing is people have been asking my opinion on coronavirus a lot and clinic yesterday

it was every conversation that came up and so I think you need to be prepared as a clinician if you want to build trust with your patients you need to know a bit about this virus it's a huge topic so what are you going to tell them how and then second layer more importantly as a public health perspective what are you actually doing your clinic to prevent the spread of the virus and there are a couple of things I think people have got wrong on social media and there is this these memes going around of it's just it's a load of noise generated by the media and it's not really a threat and there's a lot of stuff like that which is downplaying the potential threat and I understand it because one of the things that comes up in these memes as the numbers have appeared small for so long so if there are 40 people infected in China on one day it's why we worrying about 40 people in China we're a long way away and there's so few of them what I think people are missing is this the exponential threat so if you picture someone with flu as being a bit like a domino with seasonal flu the our number is close to one so if one person gets

the flu they tend to pass it on to one other from a domino perspective one domino falls over knocks over the next domino each fall of a domino is a transition a transmission cycle and so you could say after fifteen transmission cycles 15 people will have flu the difference with the corona virus is that one domino effectively knocks over three other dominoes if the tribes if the our number is three it's usually about two point five to three that I've seen mentioned in the studies that means there's a completely different result when you get to the end of 15 cycles in fact at the end of 15 transmission cycles with the corona virus it's about four to five million people who have the virus and that puts us in a whole different world of risk I don't think people are very good at anticipating exponential changes it's a little bit like mobile phones when they were first adopted we had one girl at our school who had one and everyone thought she was a bit weird and then literally a year later you had to have a mobile phone and no one saw that coming I mean even the companies at that time didn't predict it they were saying Oh an adoption of a few percent a year that's because it's an exponential change these changes that

happen in that way our brains are not geared up for and in fact if you look at an exponential graph in small sections it looks like a straight line and this what it looks like if you look at the number of cases in the UK was 40 then it was 80 that doesn't seem like a big difference but that those mathematics played out over a few weeks can turn into Millions so if you what I realized about this exponential component the fact that it's growing so fast is that if you want to respond properly to the coronavirus you actually have to overreact to react properly because it's changing so fast that our activity now to limit spread will seem like why are we doing it there's only three cases in Manchester in fact it's more than three now I think already but it's by the time it gets to the point it's an actual threat it's too late like you haven't taken the steps you've probably already passed it on someone in your buildings passed it on so I think we should start acting like it's already everywhere in order to prevent it everywhere and some people think it's going to be too hard to do that because we don't live in a dictatorship where we can weld people into their buildings

like they're doing in China but that is what controls the are number so if you want to decrease the number of spread you need to limit the number of exposures to other people and you can do that when you can barricade up roads and close people into their buildings but we're never going to do that in the UK we're going to probably stop certain events and maybe limits schools might be closed for example that'll make a significant difference the goal is to get that number to less than one because as soon as it's less than one it dies out and that's very hard to do but it's possible with good practice so simple things in your clinic are worth doing one of the things is it survives on surfaces for a fair amount of time not as long as some I've heard some people say you know up to seven days I think it's probably less than that most people saying a bit less but it does survive on particularly shiny surfaces so less well on clothing for some reason but are you should probably clean your door handles anything that's touched as people come and leave the clinic should be cleaned regularly I know we have a leaders that and are tenacious always cleaning each room and so that might really help and the other

thing would be alcohol hand gels now you can't use a lot of us use chlorhexidine paroxetine doesn't actually work so Sofia del I hope I'm saying your name right Sophie so Sophie who's a clinical pharmacist who does aesthetic shared on her page that the alcohol that you need to use alcohol in order to kill virus so if you use chlorhexidine it's actually not strong enough so those the virus is wrapped in a little plasmid layer which protects it from the environment to a degree so you need to use alcohol with over seventy percent concentration you so you can't use vodka it's got to be 70 percent and that's one other thing you can do is clean your surfaces if someone you think someone might have been slightly ill giving maybe they've come from a country and they've really arrived in your clinic that's at risk then you might want to do that spruce everything up and clean it just in case you could also send a message as to your clients to prevent those who may recently been in these high-risk areas which perhaps we can provide a link for it's on the Department of Health website gov dot UK you can google the advice about which countries are high risk you may want to advise patients who've

recently been there not to attend your clinic and you won't lose out because very few them will with everyone else one you're taking precautions so the alcohol is that when you get the normal alcohol gel is that enough yeah the normal alcohol gel is enough to kill the virus if it's on your hands and there is droplet to drop droplet spread so you might if you're close to someone it might spread it they don't think it spreads that easily not as easily as flu but it's the it's still possible if you're close to someone if you keep it 2 meter gap that's meant to be enough none of us are doing that and it's that takes you closer than that but alcohol wipes will clean your hands and surfaces well effectively what do you think about when we have boundaries in our clinic to protect against do not did not attend you know DNA's and people kind of potentially taking the mickey a little bit what do you think about if they quote coronavirus and they are within your let's say you've got a 24 hours window where you say after if you cancel within this 24 hour window before your appointment then you're gonna lose there

you know you can lose your money you deposit it or your booking fee what do you think about if they suddenly quote Corona might as well I've got a current virus or I'm worried a lot the corona virus get it I'm worried about getting the corona virus in your clinic would you forego that money that's really a tough I know the holiday companies are not giving way so if you've booked a cruise they still saying you need to come in the cruise as long as they're operating the cruise you need to come on it or you lose your money so there's something in that having said that what you don't want is someone with the corona virus turning up to your clinic and that's not unlikely that we could get to that point it is spreading fairly quickly so um I would probably say you've got me there maybe hold on for the deposit reschedule the next appointment but it's um I wouldn't want to be putting any incentive for someone who's actually sick to come to the clinic would you be wearing a mask in your clinic so one of the reasons you might do that is because you're worried about it and a symptomatic spread so that from what I've read the theory is

very few cases are spread by people who aren't symptomatic but there's at least a couple of anecdotal stories where it could be so I'd be I possibly be doing that if someone there should be some reason why you're worried which shouldn't just be that they're Chinese you know that that's where it gets really sensitive you don't want to make someone feel like you're basically being racist if they've recently come from a high-risk part of China that's different but you probably would say it rather not see you at all I think the real defense is that you isolate the risks before they come to your clinic and you just advise them to come back within two weeks later so anyone who's had it who's come from a high-risk area or has a contact who's been in high risk area or someone who's been who's had actually had the illness who's been near them you should not see those people in your clinic once they're in front of you wearing a mask it might help it's only going to be a small difference apparently if the masks get wet and it's worse we're interestingly so the virus obviously dissolves into the whatever is on the mask and then you're more likely to get

it and you need a really good quality masks have a really tight seal which most people don't have it's probably better at keeping the virus in if you've already got the virus then it is about keeping the virus out so they're not that amazing you could wear them though there's not it's not unreasonable to wear them in a clinical environment anyway so I hope you found that useful and there's something for you to go home and think about maybe something you can improve and maybe I've given you some insight on how things really work it would be really great to hear your comments below so leave us your thoughts and share this if you think it's helpful and we will see you on the next episode take care [Music] you
